quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Torque"?

options.text: v_esc = √(2GM/r); the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field entirely (total energy = 0)
τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters

quiz_questions.explanation: "Torque" is defined as: τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's Second Law for rotation"?

options.text: An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work
τ_net = Iα; net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's Second Law for rotation" is defined as: τ_net = Iα; net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Moment arm (lever arm)"?

options.text: r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force
F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled
L = Iω; the rotational analog of linear momentum, measured in kg·m²/s
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma

quiz_questions.explanation: "Moment arm (lever arm)" is defined as: r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Static equilibrium"?

options.text: When τ_net,ext = 0: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂; if I decreases, ω increases proportionally
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α
W_net = ½mv² - ½mv₀²; relates the total work done by all forces to the change in kinetic energy
ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: "Static equilibrium" is defined as: ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sign convention for torque"?

options.text: Counterclockwise torques are positive; clockwise torques are negative (right-hand rule convention)
F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sign convention for torque" is defined as: Counterclockwise torques are positive; clockwise torques are negative (right-hand rule convention). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m"?

options.text: Tension
Critical speed
Total orbital energy
Torque

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Torque".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "τ_net = Iα; net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration"?

options.text: Angular momentum (particle)
Geosynchronous orbit
Newton's Second Law for rotation
Unit vector

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's Second Law for rotation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force"?

options.text: Work by a spring
Moment arm (lever arm)
Center of mass (discrete)
Linear momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Moment arm (lever arm)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration"?

options.text: Energy dissipation
Normal force on incline
Net force
Static equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Static equilibrium".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Counterclockwise torques are positive; clockwise torques are negative (right-hand rule convention)"?

options.text: Instantaneous power
Static friction
Sign convention for torque
Center of mass (discrete)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sign convention for torque".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Torque and Rotational Dynamics is accurate?

options.text: For constant acceleration, the kinematic equations (v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at², v² = v₀² + 2a(x - x₀)) relate these quantities.
Real collisions are partially inelastic.
In vertical circles, the net centripetal force changes with position because gravity's radial component varies.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Torque and Rotational Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: In perfectly inelastic collisions, the objects stick together and maximum KE is lost (converted to thermal/sound energy).
Torque about a pivot determines whether an object rotates and in which direction.
Geosynchronous orbits, escape velocity (v_esc = √(2GM/r)), and energy in orbits (E = -GMm/2r) are key applications of gravitational and energy concepts.
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Torque and Rotational Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: Power analysis is critical for engine design, electrical systems, and athletic performance.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
Static equilibrium requires both ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0, providing the basis for analyzing bridges, seesaws, and structural systems.
AP Physics C emphasizes calculus-based kinematics, using derivatives and integrals to describe motion with non-constant acceleration.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Torque and Rotational Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.
Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Torque and Rotational Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
Rotational Inertia (moment of inertia, I) quantifies an object's resistance to angular acceleration, analogous to mass in linear motion.
Torque about a pivot determines whether an object rotates and in which direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Torque and Rotational Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium
Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey
CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Torque and Rotational Dynamics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Engine crankshaft design optimizing moment of inertia to smooth out power delivery from individual cylinder firings
Door handle placement: handles are at the edge (maximum r) to maximize torque for a given applied force, making doors easy to open
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Torque and Rotational Dynamics?

options.text: Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Ice skater spins: decreasing I by pulling arms in causes ω to increase to conserve L; extending arms slows the spin
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Torque and Rotational Dynamics in practice?

options.text: Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Analyzing the work required to stretch a bungee cord by integrating the non-linear elastic force over the extension distance
Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Torque and Rotational Dynamics.
